title: Reading livestock body language
subtitle: The universal posture and movement signals that indicate health, stress, or pain across species.
excerpt: Livestock that are unwell, in pain, or under severe stress show recognisable physical signs regardless of species. A sheep, goat, pig, or cow that holds its body differently from the rest of the group, avoids movement, separates from the herd, or shows changes in posture is communicating a problem. Training the observation habit to look for these signals makes early detection automatic.
sourceNotes: Grandin (2010), Improving Animal Welfare: a practical approach, CABI. DEFRA Animal Welfare Codes (multiple species). Mellor et al. (2020), Animals Journal: pain assessment in livestock.

glossaryTerms.definition: A characteristic body position or movement pattern associated with acute or chronic pain in livestock. Common signs across species include: back arched or hunched; head held low or extended; weight shifting off an affected limb; reduced movement and slow gait; flank watching or repeated looking at the abdomen (in abdominal pain); teeth grinding (bruxism, more visible in sheep and goats than in pigs).

A consistent early indicator of illness or injury in herd or flock species. Prey animals maintain group membership as a survival strategy; an animal that self-isolates is signalling that its condition prevents it from keeping up. A sheep at the back of the moving flock, a pig lying while others are active at feed time, or a goat refusing to come to the gate are all separation signs.

content.text: Livestock communicate through body posture and behaviour, not vocalisation. The signals are often subtle in prey species, which have evolved to conceal weakness. Developing the habit of reading these signals takes practice but becomes automatic quickly once you have learned what normal looks like for each species you keep.
Separation: the most reliable early signal
Across all herd and flock species,

is the first observable sign of a problem in the majority of cases. Look for it at feeding time, when the whole group is motivated to move. An animal that does not rise when the others do, or that lags 10 metres behind the group, is the first animal to investigate.
Once you have identified a candidate animal, assess its posture.

in livestock is consistent across species: the animal curves into itself (arched back), weight shifts off the painful area, movement is minimal and deliberate, and the head may hang low. A grinding jaw movement, especially in sheep and goats, is a strong indicator of significant pain. Abdominal pain causes repeated looking at the flank, an animal that cannot settle, and in horses and some cattle, sweating and pawing.

A dull or staring coat (rough, raised, lacking the shine of a healthy animal) points to a chronic problem: poor nutrition, systemic disease, or internal parasites. This is a slow signal rather than an acute one, but it is useful when assessing whether an animal is doing well. Compare coats across the group: the one that looks clearly worse is the one to investigate.

Bright, alert eyes with no discharge are normal. Sunken eyes indicate dehydration. Half-closed eyes in an animal that is not relaxed and resting are a sign of significant illness. Bilateral nasal discharge in a housed flock or herd is a respiratory alert; clear discharge in cold weather from one or two animals is less significant than thick yellow or green discharge affecting multiple animals.
